excerpt: Human Rights Watch concludes that as few as 489 and as many as 528 Yugoslav civilians were killed in 90 incidents during the NATO air campaign.
pages_or_sections: summary section; lines 47-51 in tool view
transform_note: The best estimate is the midpoint of the published low and high counts.
confidence_note: High confidence for a campaign-wide civilian fatality band.

excerpt: The commemorative B92/Tanjug report gives a military and police death toll of 1,008, broken down as 659 soldiers and 349 policemen.
pages_or_sections: main text; lines 140-149 in tool view
transform_note: Police are kept in the military-side numerator because the episode concerns state security personnel exposed to the bombardment campaign.
confidence_note: Medium confidence. The figure is specific and useful but comes from retrospective official commemorative reporting.

excerpt: Human Rights Watch reports that the Yugoslav Army had about 85,000 to 114,000 total personnel, with around 20,000 in Kosovo by late April 1999.
transform_note: The low and high active-force bounds are taken directly from HRW; 100,000 is used as the central active-force scenario.
confidence_note: Medium confidence for campaign-period active-force magnitude.

excerpt: The same HRW source notes an estimate of 7,000 men for the PJP and cites a UK MOD claim of 5,000 members plus 8,000 reservists.
transform_note: Used as the bounded special-police component added to the army scenario.
confidence_note: Medium confidence for an order-of-magnitude special-police contribution.

excerpt: The CRS report dates NATO air operations from 24 March 1999 and says the campaign targeted airfields, air defense and communications centers, barracks, logistics, ground forces in Kosovo, and national infrastructure in Serbia and Kosovo before suspension on 10 June.
pages_or_sections: pp. 4-5; lines 152-155 and 188-199 in tool view
transform_note: Used to justify keeping one campaign-level episode rather than forcing a local denominator onto a campaign-wide target set.
confidence_note: High confidence for the campaign geometry and timing.

excerpt: The 1999 World Factbook estimate gives the FRY a population of 11,206,847 and notes that wartime dislocation affects population precision.
pages_or_sections: country profile; lines 71-72 in tool view
transform_note: Used as the civilian-stock baseline for the campaign-level denominator.
confidence_note: Medium confidence for a near-contemporaneous national stock figure.

excerpt: Human Rights Watch documents the expulsion of 862,979 ethnic Albanians during the period of NATO bombing and broader mass displacement in Kosovo.
transform_note: Used only to build conservative civilian-stock scenarios: full subtraction, half subtraction, or no subtraction.
confidence_note: High confidence that displacement was massive; lower confidence for converting it into an average FRY-wide exposure stock.
